mnemonics.title: Counting Sort Steps
mnemonics.content: C-P-S: Count occurrences → Cumulative sum → Place in sorted order. Remember: 'Counting Properly Sorts' - like counting inventory then arranging items on shelves.

mnemonics.title: Time Complexity
mnemonics.content: O(n + k) - Think 'n items plus k range'. Like counting votes: count each vote (n) plus check all candidates (k). Linear when range equals elements.

mnemonics.title: Space Complexity
mnemonics.content: O(n + k) - Need input, output, and count array. Like needing: shopping list (input), cart (output), and shelf inventory (count).

acronyms.usage: Determines count array size and efficiency of Counting Sort. When k = O(n), sort is optimal.

acronyms.expansion: Number of elements in input array
acronyms.usage: Used with k in time complexity O(n + k)
Phase 1: Count frequency of each element using count array as histogram.
Phase 2: Place elements in output array using cumulative count to determine positions.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a histogram where each bar height = count of that value. After cumulative, it becomes a running total height chart showing positions.
Think of numbered parking slots. Count array tells us the last slot number for each value. We fill from right to left to maintain stability.
Like shifting a number line left so all values become positive. If min = -5, element -3 becomes index (-3 - (-5)) = 2.

analogies.analogy: Like census taking: first count how many people in each age group (counting phase), then use that to assign seat numbers in an auditorium (placement phase).
If sorting 100 students' test scores (0-100), k=101 is manageable. But if sorting 100 students' IDs (1 to 1 billion), you'd need a huge workspace - inefficient!
In a queue of people with same ticket number, stable sort keeps them in their original queue order, like first-come-first-served within same priority.
